title: Citizenship · Principles of American Democracy
description: The Constitution, Declaration of Independence, the Bill of Rights, and the rule of law (2026 civics test, 128-question version).

cards.front: What is the supreme law of the land?
cards.back: The Constitution. It is the highest law in the United States; every other law and government action must agree with it. No statute, executive order, or court ruling can override it.
distractors: The Declaration of Independence.
The Bill of Rights.
The Federalist Papers.
Acts passed by Congress.
Rulings of the Supreme Court.

cards.back: It sets up the government, defines the government, and protects the basic rights of Americans. Any one of these three is an acceptable answer on the test.
cards.quizAnswer: Sets up and defines the government, and protects Americans' basic rights.
distractors: Declares the colonies' independence from Great Britain and Britain's king.
Lists the grievances of the colonists against King George III.
Frees the enslaved people and abolishes slavery across the nation.
Establishes the federal income tax and the national banking system.
Grants women and citizens of every race the right to vote.

cards.front: The idea of self-government is in the first three words of the Constitution. What are these words?
cards.back: "We the People." These opening words of the Preamble mean the government's power comes from the people, not from a king.
distractors: Life, liberty, happiness.
We hold these truths.
One nation, indivisible.
Of the people, by.
All men created equal.

cards.back: A change, or an addition, to the Constitution. Amendments let the Constitution be updated over time without rewriting it.
cards.quizAnswer: A change or addition to the Constitution.
distractors: A law passed by both houses of Congress.
A ruling by the U.S. Supreme Court.
An order signed by the President.
A treaty made with a foreign nation.
A right listed in the Declaration of Independence.

cards.front: What do we call the first ten amendments to the Constitution?
cards.back: The Bill of Rights. Ratified in 1791, these first ten amendments protect basic individual freedoms such as speech, religion, and the right to a fair trial.
distractors: The Articles of Confederation.
The Declaration of Independence.
The Federalist Papers.
The Reconstruction Amendments.
The Preamble to the Constitution.

cards.front: What is one right or freedom from the First Amendment?
cards.back: Speech, religion, assembly, press, or petition the government. The First Amendment protects all five; naming any one is correct.
cards.quizAnswer: Freedom of speech (also religion, assembly, press, or petition).
distractors: The right to keep and bear arms.
The right to a fast and fair trial.
Protection against unreasonable searches.
The right to vote in elections.
The right against self-incrimination.

cards.front: How many amendments does the Constitution have?
cards.back: Twenty-seven (27). The first ten are the Bill of Rights; the most recent, the 27th, was ratified in 1992.
distractors: Thirteen (13).
Twenty-five (25).
Twenty-six (26).
Fifty (50).

cards.front: What did the Declaration of Independence do?
cards.back: It announced and declared the United States' independence from Great Britain. Adopted July 4, 1776, it said the colonies were free states no longer ruled by the British king.
cards.quizAnswer: Announced and declared independence from Great Britain.
distractors: Set up the structure of the federal government.
Listed the basic rights protected for all citizens.
Created the three branches of the government.
Ended slavery throughout the original colonies.
Established the United States Supreme Court.

cards.front: What are two rights in the Declaration of Independence?
cards.back: Life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness. The Declaration says all people are created equal and have these unalienable rights.
cards.quizAnswer: Life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.
distractors: Speech, religion, and the right to assemble.
Voting, jury service, and running for office.
Freedom of the press and the right to petition.
The right to bear arms and a fair trial.
Equal protection and due process of law.

cards.back: You can practice any religion, or not practice a religion. The government cannot make you follow a particular faith, and it cannot stop you from worshiping as you choose.
cards.quizAnswer: You can practice any religion, or practice no religion at all.
distractors: The government chooses one official national religion.
Every citizen must belong to a recognized church.
Only the religions named in the Constitution are allowed.
Religion may be practiced only inside private homes.
Citizens may criticize the government without any punishment.

cards.front: What is the economic system of the United States?
cards.back: A capitalist economy, also called a market economy. Prices and production are largely set by private businesses and consumers rather than by the government.
distractors: A socialist, state-planned economy.
A communist, collective economy.
A purely agricultural barter economy.
A feudal, land-based economy.
A government-owned command economy.

cards.back: Everyone must follow the law; leaders and the government must obey the law; no one is above the law. The law applies equally to all people, including those in power.
cards.quizAnswer: Everyone, including leaders and government, must follow the law; no one is above it.
distractors: The President has the final word on every law.
Only judges and police are required to obey laws.
Laws apply to ordinary citizens but not officials.
The majority can set aside the law when needed.
Each state writes laws that ignore the Constitution.
